nativeRange.habitat: Warm, shallow, densely vegetated lowland waters, including slow-moving rivers, backwaters, lagoons, drainage ditches, and coastal marshes. The species tolerates slightly brackish conditions in coastal areas.

decorPreferences: dense surface vegetation and floating plants (water lettuce, Amazon frogbit) for cover
emergent plants and tall background vegetation
driftwood and branches near the surface
minimal open water — heavily planted periphery with open central swimming lane

diet.notes: The Pike Livebearer is functionally an obligate piscivore: its wild diet consists almost entirely of live fish, with large invertebrates taken occasionally. In captivity it requires a steady supply of live feeder fish — guppies, mollies, and platies are commonly used. Some individuals can be conditioned over time to accept large frozen-thawed whole fish such as silversides or lance fish, or chunked raw fish flesh, but many stubbornly refuse anything that is not actively moving. Feeding dead or prepared foods is unreliable, and specimens that refuse to convert may starve. Gut-loaded, nutritionally complete feeder fish should be used wherever possible to avoid nutritional deficiencies. Feeder fish sourced from outside the aquarist's own stock carry a significant disease risk; breeding a dedicated feeder colony is strongly preferred.

goodWith: no tankmates recommended in a community setting
large robust livebearers of similar size in a species-only setup (with caution — cannibalism risk if size disparity exists)
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed (virtually all common community fish)
guppies, mollies, platies, tetras, rasboras, danios, corydoras
juveniles with adults of the same species

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Belonesox belizanus. Standard FishBase species photo showing the characteristic elongated body and pike-like snout.

summary: The Pike Livebearer (Belonesox belizanus) is the only large piscivore in the family Poeciliidae, reaching up to 20 cm with an elongated, pike-like body. Native to Central American lowlands and established as an invasive species in Florida, it demands live feeder fish and a species-only setup.
careNotes:
The Pike Livebearer (Belonesox belizanus) is a uniquely specialized member of the family Poeciliidae — the only species in the genus Belonesox and the sole large obligate piscivore in a family otherwise composed of micro-feeders and omnivores. It carries a distinctly elongated, pike-like body and a large upturned mouth lined with small but sharp teeth, built for surface ambush rather than active pursuit. Females are substantially larger than males, reaching up to 20 cm, while males typically top out near 10–12 cm — a size difference that is visible at a glance.

A tank of at least 200 L with a 120 cm footprint is the practical minimum for a single adult female; a breeding pair or small group requires 300 L or more. As a surface ambush predator that lurks motionless among dense vegetation before striking, the species benefits from heavily planted tanks with abundant floating cover. An open central lane allows cruising while planted edges and surface vegetation provide naturalistic ambush zones and reduce stress.

Water conditions should be warm (24–30°C), moderately hard, and alkaline (pH 7.0–8.5, gH 10–25). Native to coastal lowland habitats that can be slightly brackish, the species tolerates a modest salt addition (SG up to approximately 1.003), which can help reduce disease pressure. Good filtration and regular water changes are essential, particularly given the high-protein live-fish diet that generates significant waste.

The primary challenge in keeping the Pike Livebearer is diet: it is functionally a live-fish specialist. Aquarist-bred guppies, mollies, or platies make ideal feeder stock. Some individuals can be conditioned over time to accept large frozen silversides or lance fish through gradual hunger conditioning and movement simulation — for example, offering food on a feeding stick — but conversion to dead foods is not guaranteed, and many specimens will not make the switch. Feeder fish sourced from outside the aquarist's own stock carry significant disease risk; establishing a dedicated feeder colony is the responsible approach.

Conspecific aggression and cannibalism are real concerns. Males are frequently harassed by females, and any meaningful size difference between specimens can result in predation. Juveniles and adults must be separated immediately.

This species has established invasive populations in South Florida through aquarium releases into warm canals and ditches. Specimens must never be released into the wild.
breedingNotes: The Pike Livebearer is a livebearer (viviparous) and reproduces in the typical poeciliid fashion — females give birth to fully formed live fry. Gestation lasts approximately 4–6 weeks at optimal temperatures. Brood sizes are relatively small for a livebearer of this size, typically 20–100 fry depending on female size and age; larger, older females produce larger broods. Fry are born large by livebearer standards — often 1.5–2 cm — and are immediately capable predators, attempting to hunt from birth. Fry must be separated from parents immediately as both the female and male will consume them. A dedicated fry tank with small live food (newly hatched brine shrimp, small guppy fry) is required from day one. The male must typically be removed after mating to prevent female predation on him. Females can store sperm and produce multiple broods from a single mating. Selective breeding programs are rare given the specialist husbandry demands.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Belonesox belizanus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Atlantic slope lowlands from Mexico to Nicaragua), adult size (males to 10 cm, females to 20 cm), temperature range (26–30°C), pH (7.0–8.0), hardness tolerances, piscivorous diet details, breeding notes (gestation ~28 days, brood 20–100 fry), tank requirements, and conspecific aggression notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Belonesox belizanus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Poeciliidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (females to 20.5 cm TL, males smaller), native range (Mexico through Central America), invasive range (Florida, USA), diet (piscivore), and habitat (fresh and slightly brackish vegetated lowland waters). Confirmed monotypic genus Belonesox.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pike Killifish (Pike Livebearer)
additional.notes: Care guide confirming live-feeder dependency, specialist-only suitability, species-only tank requirement, size (females to 8 inches/20 cm), and temperature range (75–86°F / 24–30°C). Noted difficulty converting to frozen/prepared foods and invasive status in Florida.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Belonesox belizanus profile
additional.notes: Supplementary UK hobby source. Confirmed piscivore classification, livebearer biology, specialist care level, Central American origin, and the species\' distinction as the only large predatory poeciliid. Notes on feeding difficulty and aggression consistent with other sources.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Pike Livebearer species overview
additional.notes: Historical hobby source. Noted unique evolutionary position of Belonesox as the apex piscivore of the family Poeciliidae, live-fish feeding requirement, Florida invasive status, and breeding observations including large fry size at birth.

fish.breedingNotes: Gestation runs approximately 4–6 weeks at optimal temperatures, with broods of 20–100 fry depending on the female's size and age. Fry are born large for a livebearer, often around 1.5–2 cm, and are immediately predatory. Both parents will consume fry, so separation is required at birth, and the male should typically be removed after mating to prevent injury from the female. Fry require live foods from day one.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Live-feeder-only piscivorous livebearer reaching 20cm. Will hunt and consume tankmates. Single-fish or species-only setup. Invasive in Florida via aquarium releases.
